outcomes.title: Reduce purchase approval delays
outcomes.description: Compress the elapsed time between a staff member submitting a purchase request and receiving a decision, so operational needs are met without staff resorting to workarounds. Approval queues, urgent flagging, delegation, and escalation tools remove the wait caused by absent or overloaded approvers.
successIndicators: Average time from PO submission to first manager decision drops measurably quarter-over-quarter
Percentage of requests cleared within a target SLA (e.g., 48 hours) rises across manager and owner queues
Volume of POs sitting in a manager or owner queue beyond a set aging threshold falls month-over-month
Number of escalations initiated because an approver was unreachable decreases as delegation is used proactively
Staff-reported instances of purchasing delays blocking operational work decline in periodic check-ins

outcomes.title: Improve purchasing visibility
outcomes.description: Give managers, finance, and ownership a real-time, role-appropriate view of every purchase request at every stage — from draft through closed — so no spend commitment is invisible until after the fact. The Registry, role-scoped dashboards, and Reports surface a single version of the truth.
successIndicators: Proportion of active POs visible to the appropriate approver within their queue or registry without manual inquiry rises toward 100%
Time to answer the question 'what is outstanding with this vendor right now?' drops from days to minutes using the vendor directory and registry filters
Owner dashboard accurately reflects total authorized spend quarter-to-date without requiring a manual spreadsheet reconciliation
Number of surprise invoices for purchases not tracked in PO.vin decreases quarter-over-quarter
Finance manager can produce a current open-PO aging report from the Reports page without requesting a data extract from IT

outcomes.title: Increase purchasing accountability
outcomes.description: Create an unambiguous record of who requested, who approved, who paid, and who received every purchase, so responsibility can never be diffused across informal channels. The per-PO Activity tab, Audit tab, and audit-bundle CSV make every decision and its author traceable.
successIndicators: Every purchase order has a named requester, a named approving manager, and a named owner or bypass record — zero anonymous decisions
Percentage of POs with a complete justification and business description meeting the minimum-character requirement trends toward 100%
Number of 'who approved this?' inquiries fielded by the comptroller or finance team drops significantly
Separation-of-duties violations caught and rejected by the system (SOD_VIOLATION events) are visible and reviewed monthly
Audit bundle CSVs are available on demand for any PO, eliminating the need to reconstruct approval history from email threads

outcomes.title: Reduce manual procurement administration
outcomes.description: Replace the email chains, spreadsheet trackers, and verbal approvals that staff use today to move purchase requests forward. PO.vin's wizard, automatic routing, threshold-based stage skipping, notification rules, and bulk CSV imports absorb the coordination work that otherwise falls on individuals.
successIndicators: Time staff spend per week chasing approval status via email or messaging falls measurably after go-live
Number of purchase requests tracked in spreadsheets outside PO.vin drops to zero within a defined transition period
Admin time spent on manual user and vendor onboarding drops after bulk CSV import capability is used for initial data load
Notification rules eliminate the need for manual email reminders when POs cross a workflow stage
Managers report spending less time reconstructing PO history before making an approval decision

outcomes.title: Improve spend control before purchases occur
outcomes.description: Enforce a structured review and authorization gate on every purchase commitment before a vendor is engaged or an invoice is received. Configurable approval thresholds, two-stage manager-then-owner routing, and budget-category tagging ensure the right eyes see the right requests before money moves.
successIndicators: Zero purchases exceeding the owner-approval threshold are authorized without an owner approval or a recorded bypass
Percentage of POs with a budget category assigned at submission rises to near 100% within a quarter of go-live
Spend authorized without an approval record (off-system purchases) drops measurably after rollout
Number of POs returned via the clarification cycle for insufficient justification or missing cost detail trends downward as staff learn the standard
Owner approval queue response time stays within the configured SLA, evidenced by dashboard and Reports data

outcomes.title: Standardize purchasing processes
outcomes.description: Replace department-specific or person-specific purchasing habits with a single, documented workflow that all staff follow. The five-step PO wizard enforces consistent fields, classifications, and justification standards; threshold settings apply uniformly across the organization.
successIndicators: All purchase requests originate through the PO wizard — zero requests processed via email approval alone
Line-item classification rates (asset / consumable / service / software / subscription / other) are populated on 100% of submitted POs within one quarter
Variance in required field completeness across departments falls to near zero (e.g., business justification ≥ 20 characters on all submissions)
New staff can submit a correctly structured purchase request without one-on-one coaching, using the wizard's built-in step validation
Threshold configuration is reviewed and updated on a defined schedule, documented in Admin Settings rather than informal policy memos

outcomes.title: Reduce purchasing bottlenecks
outcomes.description: Prevent individual approvers from becoming single points of failure that stall entire request queues. Delegation lets a manager hand authority to a peer when unavailable; escalation pushes a stalled PO to the owner stage; urgent flagging surfaces time-sensitive requests at the top of the queue.
successIndicators: Number of POs aging beyond an SLA threshold in any single approver's queue falls after delegation and escalation features are adopted
Delegation events per quarter track as a ratio of total approvals — meaningful use indicates the feature is preventing stalls rather than being ignored
Manager Review Queue empties to zero pending items at least once per week for each active manager
Urgent-flagged POs receive a first decision within a tighter SLA than standard requests, measurable in the Reports stage-timing data
No single approver holds more than a defined percentage of all in-flight approvals at any one time

outcomes.title: Improve supplier request tracking
outcomes.description: Maintain a structured record of every purchase request associated with each vendor — covering all open, in-progress, and historical POs — so supplier relationships and commitments are managed proactively rather than reconstructed from invoices. The vendor directory with inline PO history and the Registry's vendor filter deliver this view.
successIndicators: Any staff member or manager can pull the complete history of POs against a specific vendor from the vendor directory in under a minute
Duplicate-vendor banners surface prior POs to the same supplier at the point of a new request, reducing unintentional double-ordering
Vendor relationship owners are assigned in the directory, creating clear accountability for each supplier relationship
Open PO aging CSV from Reports identifies vendors with overdue receipt or unresolved payment, allowing proactive follow-up
Number of vendor-related disputes attributable to missing or misrouted purchase records drops year-over-year

outcomes.title: Improve purchasing compliance
outcomes.description: Ensure that the organization's purchasing policy — including approval tiers, separation-of-duties requirements, and documentation standards — is enforced by the system rather than relying on individual memory or manual checks. PO.vin's threshold engine, SOD controls, and required-field validation make policy structural.
successIndicators: Separation-of-duties rejections are logged and reviewed; the count of attempted violations trends downward as staff learn the rules
Bypassed Approvals queue is reviewed on a defined cycle, with no bypass left unacknowledged beyond a set window
Percentage of finalized POs with all GL accounts assigned reaches 100% — the Finalize gate enforces this structurally
Policy-required attachments (quotes, contracts, proof of delivery) are present on the required PO types at a defined completeness rate
Internal or external audit findings related to purchasing compliance that reference missing documentation or unauthorized approvals drop to zero

outcomes.title: Increase approval turnaround speed
outcomes.description: Reduce the clock time from when a purchase request enters an approval queue to when it exits with a decision. Notification rules alert approvers immediately on queue entry; urgent flagging and dashboard stat tiles keep pending counts visible; the mobile-accessible approval interface means decisions happen in the field, not just at a desk.
successIndicators: Median manager-queue decision time (approve / reject / clarify) drops measurably in the first quarter after go-live
Owner-queue median decision time on high-value requests stays within a defined target, tracked on the owner dashboard
Percentage of POs receiving a first-touch decision within 24 hours of submission rises above a defined threshold
Notification emails (triggered by workflow-event rules) are confirmed delivered and opened within the session window by approvers
Reports stage-timing chart shows consistent improvement in Pending Manager and Pending Owner dwell times quarter-over-quarter

outcomes.title: Improve procurement audit readiness
outcomes.description: Ensure that at any moment — including mid-cycle — the organization can produce a complete, tamper-evident record of every purchase decision, payment, receipt, and asset creation without manual reconstruction. The per-PO Audit tab, audit-bundle CSV, and Activity timeline make this instantaneous.
successIndicators: Any individual PO's full audit bundle can be exported to CSV from the PO detail in under two minutes, covering every governed mutation
No gaps exist in the audit timeline — every approve, reject, clarify, pay, and receive action is recorded with the acting user and timestamp
Year-end or spot-check audit requests are satisfied without any manual compilation from email or spreadsheets
Registry CSV exports are available on demand and include all filter dimensions (status, department, amount, date range)
Bypassed Approvals records show the threshold-routing rationale for every auto-cleared PO, satisfying auditor questions about missing owner sign-off

outcomes.title: Reduce unauthorized purchasing
outcomes.description: Eliminate purchases committed outside the approval workflow — whether through verbal authority, personal credit cards, or direct vendor invoices — by making the PO wizard the required path for every purchase commitment. Separation-of-duties controls and the owner-approval gate close the gaps that allow unauthorized spend.
successIndicators: Invoices received from vendors without a corresponding approved PO in PO.vin drop to zero within a defined transition period
Credit card or petty-cash purchases that bypass the PO workflow are formally categorized, tracked, and subject to a separate policy with defined limits
Separation-of-duties violations caught by the system are reviewed and addressed; no SOD bypass goes unlogged
All staff with purchasing authority are active in PO.vin with their manager assignment set, so no requests are routed informally
Post-period audits find no approved expenses that lack a PO record in the system

outcomes.title: Improve budget adherence
outcomes.description: Connect every purchase request to a budget category before authorization so that spending against each budget line is visible and defensible throughout the period — not just at period close. Budget-category tagging on every PO line, combined with the spend-by-category report, provides a running picture of consumption.
successIndicators: 100% of submitted POs carry a budget category by the end of the first quarter of use
Spend-by-category report from Reports shows variance to plan on a monthly review cadence without manual reconciliation
Number of over-budget surprises discovered at period close falls measurably after real-time category tracking is in place
Finance manager can produce a budget-versus-authorized-spend view for any department from Reports without a data request
Owner approval of high-value requests consistently references the budget category and available balance, visible in PO comments and justifications

outcomes.title: Improve purchasing transparency
outcomes.description: Make the status, value, and history of every purchase request visible to the people who need it — requesters tracking their own submissions, managers seeing their team's spend, and owners reviewing cross-department activity — without anyone having to ask. Role-scoped dashboards, the Registry, and real-time status badges deliver this without oversharing.
successIndicators: Requesters can see the current stage of every PO they submitted from My Requests without contacting their manager
Managers can view all POs for their direct reports across all stages from the Registry with a single department filter
Owner dashboard stat tiles accurately reflect awaiting-sign-off and QTD authorized spend without a manual pull
Number of status-inquiry messages sent outside PO.vin (email, chat) drops measurably in the first 60 days
Finance and accounting staff can identify any PO's payment status from the Registry In Accounting or In Receive tab in under 30 seconds

outcomes.title: Reduce procurement errors
outcomes.description: Catch mistakes — wrong vendor, missing justification, incorrect line classification, absent GL account, duplicate order — before they become committed purchases or accounting corrections. The PO wizard's step validation, duplicate-vendor banners, Finalize GL gate, and dry-run CSV import pattern prevent errors at the source.
successIndicators: Percentage of POs rejected at Finalize due to missing GL accounts drops to zero within two quarters as staff learn the standard
Duplicate-vendor banners are acknowledged on every PO submitted to a vendor with prior history, reducing unintentional double-orders
Number of POs requiring a clarification cycle for missing or invalid information falls quarter-over-quarter
CSV bulk-import dry-run errors are resolved before commit, producing a zero-error commit rate as staff learn the templates
Finance team reports fewer journal-entry corrections attributable to misclassified PO lines or wrong GL assignments

outcomes.title: Improve purchasing productivity
outcomes.description: Let staff spend less time navigating procurement bureaucracy and more time on their actual jobs. Auto-saving drafts, a clear five-step wizard, pre-populated requester fields, searchable vendor directory, and direct links from dashboards to queues cut the friction out of both submitting and approving requests.
successIndicators: Average time to complete and submit a new purchase request (wizard submission time) falls measurably after initial learning curve
Staff-reported satisfaction with the purchasing process improves in periodic surveys after PO.vin replaces email-based workflows
Manager time-to-decision per PO in the review queue drops as approvers become familiar with the one-click approve/reject/clarify interface
Number of abandoned drafts (saved but never submitted) decreases, indicating staff are completing requests rather than giving up mid-process
Accounting team time spent on payment-queue management per week decreases as the structured queue replaces manual tracking

outcomes.title: Create complete purchasing audit trails
outcomes.description: Ensure that the full lifecycle of every purchase — from the initial draft through every approval, hold, clarification, payment, receipt, and asset registration — is captured in an immutable, exportable record. The Activity timeline, Audit tab, and audit-bundle CSV on each PO deliver this without any additional manual logging.
successIndicators: Audit-bundle CSV for any PO contains every governed mutation with actor, timestamp, and prior/new state — zero gaps
Activity timeline on every PO shows color-coded events for every stage transition, including bypass chips on override rows
No purchase that has reached the Paid stage lacks a corresponding receipt record or a documented reason for the gap
Asset-line POs have corresponding equipment records in the asset registry, with custody history traceable back to the originating PO
Compliance or audit requests are satisfied from PO.vin exports alone, with no need to cross-reference email or external files

outcomes.title: Improve operational governance
outcomes.description: Give ownership and finance reliable, system-enforced oversight of the purchasing function — including threshold configuration, role assignments, separation-of-duties enforcement, and bypassed-approval review — so governance is built into day-to-day operations rather than bolted on at audit time.
successIndicators: Approval threshold bands are documented in Admin Settings and reviewed on a defined schedule, with changes logged
Bypassed Approvals queue is reviewed weekly by the owner role, with zero unreviewed bypasses beyond a set window
Every active staff member has a correct role and manager assignment in Manage Hub Users, ensuring queues route accurately
Separation-of-duties policy is enforced structurally — the system rejects violations without relying on individual discipline
Admin dashboard SLA Compliance metric is populated and reviewed monthly, with targets configured for each approval stage

outcomes.title: Reduce procurement-related risk
outcomes.description: Lower the operational and financial exposure created by informal purchasing — unauthorized commitments, undocumented vendor relationships, missing receipts, and untraceable approvals. PO.vin's vendor risk tracking, SOD controls, attachment scanning, and complete audit trail reduce the surface area for procurement risk.
successIndicators: Vendor directory includes risk classifications for all active suppliers, reviewed and updated on a defined cycle
Attachment virus scanning (VirusTotal integration) prevents quarantined files from being downloaded or acted on
Number of POs with no supporting documentation (no attachments where policy requires them) trends toward zero
High-value or new-vendor POs are consistently routed to owner approval — no qualifying PO bypasses the owner stage without a recorded threshold reason
Period-end review finds no purchase commitments made without a corresponding approved PO record in the system

outcomes.title: Lower procurement operating costs
outcomes.description: Reduce the labor cost of running the purchasing function by automating routing, notifications, and record-keeping that staff currently do manually — and by surfacing spend patterns that reveal consolidation or renegotiation opportunities. Less time on administration means the same team handles higher purchase volume without adding headcount.
successIndicators: Staff hours per week spent on purchasing administration (status chasing, manual routing, document retrieval) fall measurably in the first quarter
Vendor spend CSV from Reports identifies the top vendors by spend volume, enabling proactive volume-discount conversations
Accounting team processes the same or greater volume of payment-queue items without additional headcount after go-live
Cost per purchase order processed (labor time × hourly rate) decreases as wizard and automated routing replace manual steps
Number of rush or emergency purchases (urgent-flagged POs as a percentage of total) falls as better pipeline visibility enables earlier requisition

committee.name: Owner / Managing Director
interests: Knowing that no purchase commitment can be made without her explicit sign-off above a set dollar threshold — enforced by the system, not by trust
A real-time view of total authorized spend across all departments without having to ask accounting for a spreadsheet
Ensuring the business never gets an invoice surprise for a purchase she didn't know was being made
The ability to see and review every PO that was auto-routed past her approval stage in the Bypassed Approvals queue
Confidence that the approval trail is clean enough to hold up to an accountant or bank asking about spending controls
Making sure urgent operational purchases aren't held up in a queue while she's traveling — delegation and escalation must work reliably
Knowing who owns each supplier relationship and what the business's total exposure to that vendor is at any moment
A system that new hires can use without extensive training so she isn't the bottleneck for procurement onboarding
Spend reporting by department and category that she can share with a bookkeeper or CPA without reformatting
Reducing the informal 'can you just buy this quickly?' requests that bypass any process and create accounting headaches later
Keeping procurement running when she's unavailable — the system should route or escalate, not stall
Understanding what the organization has already bought from a vendor before a new PO is raised to the same supplier
decisionCriteria: The owner approval queue is genuinely the second gate on high-value purchases — she can verify this by submitting a test PO above the threshold and confirming it reaches her queue before accounting sees it
Bypassed Approvals shows her every PO the threshold engine routed past her stage, with the routing reason, so nothing is invisible
Delegation lets her hand her approval authority to a trusted deputy for a defined period without losing the audit trail
The Reports spend-by-department and vendor-spend CSV give her the cross-department picture her bookkeeper needs at month-end
Setup and onboarding can be completed by the admin role without a consultant — she can't afford a long implementation
The system enforces separation of duties so the same person who requests a purchase can't approve it, even if she's unavailable
Notification rules can be configured so she gets an alert when a high-value PO hits her queue, not just when she logs in
The vendor directory holds risk classifications and prior PO history, so she knows before approving whether the vendor is new or established
Pricing is predictable on a per-user or flat-fee basis — no surprise charges when her team grows by two people
She can export the full audit bundle for any PO as a CSV and hand it to an external auditor without any explanation needed
The system works on a phone or tablet so she can action approvals from anywhere, not only from a desk
There is a clear, documented approval matrix she can point to when staff ask why a purchase requires her sign-off
goals: Never have an unplanned vendor invoice arrive for a purchase she was unaware of
Know at any moment what the organization has committed to spend and what is still pending approval
Have a clean, exportable record of every purchase decision ready for the accountant at year-end
Remove herself as a bottleneck for routine low-value requests while staying in control of high-value commitments
Give department managers autonomy to approve small purchases without creating a governance gap
Ensure new staff understand the purchasing process from day one without requiring her to explain it personally
Have supplier relationships tracked so she knows who owns each vendor and what the business owes or has committed
Reduce the time her team spends on purchasing admin so they focus on operational work instead
Be able to demonstrate to a lender, investor, or auditor that the business has real spend controls in place
Stop being copied on email threads asking for purchasing approvals — the system should handle the routing
Have the purchase workflow survive her absence — delegation and notifications must keep things moving
Catch duplicate or near-duplicate vendor orders before they become committed spend
objections: My team is small — if this adds steps to every small purchase, people will work around it instead of using it
I already have an accounting system; what does PO.vin add that a shared spreadsheet and an email approval chain can't?
Implementation cost and time — I can't afford to have my office manager tied up for weeks configuring a new system
If I'm the approver for everything above the threshold, won't I just be approving things on my phone all day?
What happens to in-flight purchases when we first go live — do I have to enter historical orders manually?
My team already forwards me emails for approvals; I'm not sure they'll switch to logging into another system
I'm worried the system forces a rigid process that doesn't accommodate the occasional urgent purchase that needs to move in an hour
If the threshold engine routes a PO past my stage automatically, how do I know I'm not missing something I should have seen?
What if someone in accounting has access they shouldn't — can they approve their own purchases?
How does this handle purchases that don't fit neatly into a category — one-off consultant fees, event costs, unusual capex?
I don't want to pay per-seat fees for users who submit one purchase a year — the pricing model needs to make sense for a small team
openQuestions: Show me exactly what I see when a PO above my threshold lands in my owner approval queue — what information is on the screen before I click approve?
If I'm away for a week, how do I delegate my approval authority to my COO without losing the audit record of who actually approved it?
Walk me through the Bypassed Approvals queue — what exactly triggers a PO to bypass my stage, and what does the bypass record show?
Can I configure different thresholds for different departments, or is it a single threshold across the whole organization?
What does the vendor directory show me about a supplier before I approve a PO to them — risk level, prior spend, open orders?
If accounting marks a PO paid but it was never received, where does that show up so I can follow up with the vendor?
How does the audit-bundle CSV look — is it readable by my CPA, or is it an internal system dump with codes I'd have to explain?
Can I see a spend-by-department report for the current quarter without running a CSV export every time?
If a manager approves a PO for one of their own purchases, does the system catch that as a separation-of-duties issue?
What happens to a PO if the manager it's routed to is deactivated in the system — does it stall, or does it escalate automatically?
Can I get a notification on my phone when a high-value PO hits my queue, without having to log in to check?
How long does it realistically take to get the system configured with our vendors, users, and threshold rules before we go live?
Is there a report that shows me every purchase we've made from a vendor over the past 12 months, so I can use it in a renegotiation?
If a staff member submits a PO with an inadequate justification, does the system catch that before it reaches my queue or does it come to me incomplete?
Walk me through what happens when I approve a PO with conditions — what does 'approved with conditions' actually mean downstream in accounting?

committee.name: CEO
interests: Ensuring the organization's procurement process reflects well on the business to lenders, investors, and professional advisors
Having a clear picture of authorized spend and pending commitments when preparing for board discussions or financing conversations
Knowing that high-value purchases require a documented business justification before they are committed, not after
Reducing the operational risk of a key approver being a single point of failure when they're traveling or ill
Receiving timely notification when a PO in his approval queue has been waiting beyond a reasonable window
Having procurement policy encoded in the system so it doesn't depend on individuals remembering the rules
Trusting that the separation-of-duties controls prevent anyone from approving their own purchases without oversight
Being able to review the organization's vendor relationships and identify concentration risk or underused suppliers
Ensuring the approval process doesn't create friction that pushes operational managers to bypass procurement entirely
A reporting view that connects purchasing activity to operational departments so he can have fact-based budget conversations with department heads
Knowing that asset purchases are tracked through receipt and registration, not just approved and forgotten
Reducing the CEO-as-bottleneck dynamic where operational spend stalls because he's unavailable
decisionCriteria: The two-stage approval structure (manager then owner) maps cleanly to how the organization actually operates, without requiring a major restructure of who reports to whom
He can configure approval thresholds that match the organization's risk appetite — not just a binary all-or-nothing gate
The system's audit trail is sufficient to satisfy external parties (auditors, bank covenants, due-diligence requests) without supplemental documentation
Delegation and escalation work reliably so operational spend doesn't stall when he or another approver is unavailable
The vendor directory and vendor-spend report give him enough visibility to have informed conversations with the finance team about supplier consolidation
The system can be deployed and adopted by the existing team without a dedicated implementation project or external consultant
Notification rules alert him when his queue has aging items, so he doesn't need to log in daily to check for stragglers
The Bypassed Approvals queue gives him retrospective visibility into what the threshold engine cleared without his direct review
Pricing is predictable and justified relative to the risk and labor cost the system replaces — not a large SaaS commitment for a small team
The Reports page gives him the cross-department spend summary he currently has to request manually from finance
Staff adoption is supported by a system that is genuinely simpler than the current email-approval workaround, not just a different interface
There is a documented role-permission model he can explain to department heads so they understand what they can and can't do
goals: Have a procurement process that demonstrates organizational maturity to external parties without requiring a dedicated procurement team
Reduce the time spent personally adjudicating purchasing requests that should be handled at the manager level
Ensure that large or unusual purchases have a paper trail that supports the business decision if it's ever questioned
Give department heads visibility into their own teams' spending without granting them access to other departments' data
Remove the operational risk of informal purchasing that creates unbudgeted liabilities
Connect purchasing activity to budget categories so financial reporting reflects real operational spend, not just invoices received
Have confidence that asset purchases are tracked from approval through receipt and registration, not lost after payment
Build a procurement foundation that scales as the organization grows without requiring a full process overhaul
Ensure that vendor relationships are documented and owned, not managed informally through personal relationships
Reduce the number of surprise invoices that arrive without a corresponding purchase record in the system
Have a reliable escalation path for urgent purchases so operational needs are met without bypassing all controls
Create conditions where finance can close each period without scrambling to account for undocumented purchases
objections: If I'm listed as an owner-stage approver, am I going to be the second bottleneck on every significant purchase in the organization?
We've tried to implement purchasing processes before and they fell apart because managers just emailed me directly — what makes adoption stick this time?
I'm concerned about the threshold configuration — if we set it too low, everything hits my queue; too high, and the control is meaningless
What's the implementation timeline, and who on my team owns the configuration — I can't spare my office manager for a long setup project
If the system routes a purchase to the wrong person because the manager assignment wasn't set correctly, who catches that?
How does this integrate with our accounting system — do we duplicate entry, or is there a data flow that reduces that work?
I worry that adding a formal system will slow down genuinely urgent purchases that need to move in a day
What happens to the existing vendor relationships and historical purchase data — do we start from scratch?
If the system goes down, what's the fallback process so operations don't stall waiting for an approval?
Separation of duties is important to me, but I also need to be pragmatic — can I grant exceptions for specific roles in specific situations?
The cost needs to justify itself against the labor we're currently spending on email-based approval coordination
openQuestions: Show me the owner approval queue — when a PO arrives requiring my sign-off, what does my screen look like and what information do I have before I decide?
Walk me through how approval thresholds are configured — can I set different bands for capital vs. expense purchases, or is it purely by dollar amount?
What does the Bypassed Approvals queue show me — specifically, what was the routing logic that cleared a PO past my stage without my review?
If I want to approve a PO but with a specific condition attached — for example, 'order only after budget confirmation' — how does that get captured and communicated to accounting?
Can I see a spend-by-department summary for the current period without exporting a CSV every time I want to check in?
How does delegation work in practice — if I assign my approval authority to the COO for two weeks, what exactly changes in the routing, and is my delegation logged?
If a manager approves a PO raised by one of their own direct reports and then also processes the payment, does the system flag that?
Walk me through the Bypassed Approvals view for a PO that was auto-cleared — what justification is recorded, and who can review it?
What's the process for a vendor that's flagged as high-risk in the directory — does that change the approval routing, or is it just informational?
Can the system generate a report showing all purchases over a threshold value in the past 12 months, suitable for a due-diligence request?
How long does initial configuration realistically take — setting up users, vendors, thresholds, and notification rules — and who does that work?
What happens if a manager approves a PO and then leaves the organization before the payment is processed — is the approval still valid, or does it require re-approval?
Can I configure the system so that new-vendor POs always route to the owner stage regardless of dollar amount?
If I want to put a department's purchasing activity on hold while we review their budget, can I do that without deactivating users?
Show me the audit-bundle CSV for a PO — what does a CPA or external auditor actually see when I hand them that file?

committee.name: COO
interests: Making sure operational purchases move through the approval workflow quickly enough that staff aren't waiting on procurement when they have work to do
Having a clear view of what each department has pending, approved, and received so she can manage operational capacity and resources accurately
Knowing that urgent-flagged POs are treated differently in the queue so time-sensitive operational needs get a faster response
Understanding how the delegation feature works so she can cover for the owner or CEO during periods when they're unavailable
Ensuring the system doesn't add so much friction that operational managers start working around it with verbal or email approvals
Tracking the status of equipment and asset purchases from approval through receipt so she knows when operational capacity is actually expanding
Having enough visibility into the manager review queues to identify when a specific manager is sitting on approvals that are blocking operations
Ensuring supplier requests tied to operational projects are tracked and can be pulled up quickly when a project team needs to reference them
Being able to see what purchases are on hold and why, so she can unblock them when the hold reason is resolved
Reducing the number of 'where is that PO?' conversations she has with managers and accounting every week
Knowing that the SLA compliance metric on the admin dashboard reflects realistic service-level expectations for each approval stage
Receiving escalations directly when a PO in the manager queue is aging beyond a reasonable window and no action has been taken
decisionCriteria: The manager review queue UI makes it fast for managers to approve, reject, or clarify a PO — ideally in under a minute for routine requests
Urgent flagging is visible in the queue and actually changes the sort order so time-critical items don't get buried
The escalation path from manager to owner is reliable and fast — she needs confidence that an unresponsive manager won't stall operations
She can see all POs for a department from the Registry with a department filter, without needing accounting-level access
The Reports stage-timing chart gives her objective data on where approvals are slowing down, not just anecdotal manager feedback
Delegation can be assigned quickly when a manager is unexpectedly unavailable — she doesn't want to wait for an admin to reconfigure routing
The system's notification rules alert managers to their queue without requiring daily logins, so approvals don't depend on check-in behavior
Asset-line POs show equipment records after receipt so she knows operational assets are registered, not just paid for and forgotten
The Receive Queue and Receive Mode give her team a clear picture of what has been paid but not yet received
Hold and Resume actions work cleanly so she can pause a purchase while verifying a budget or vendor situation without losing the PO
The system is usable on a phone or tablet so field managers can action queue items without needing to be at a desk
Manager queue scoping works correctly — each manager only sees their own direct reports' POs, not the whole organization's
goals: Eliminate the scenario where an operational purchase is delayed because it's sitting in an approver's queue unnoticed
Have a real-time view of what each department is spending and what's pending approval so she can manage budgets proactively
Make sure that urgent operational needs — equipment, supplies, services — can be approved and processed within the same business day when necessary
Remove herself as a verbal escalation path for stuck approvals by having a formal escalation function in the system
Ensure operational managers adopt the system willingly because it genuinely simplifies their approval workflow, not because they're mandated to use it
Track asset purchases from approval through registration so she has an accurate picture of operational capacity at any time
Reduce the time she spends each week fielding status questions about specific purchase orders from managers and staff
Have a reliable record of what each vendor has been contracted for, so operational project planning is based on facts not memory
Ensure the approval SLA for routine operational purchases is met consistently, measurable from Reports stage-timing data
Give the CEO confidence that operational spend is controlled without requiring her to escalate every decision
Streamline the month-end process so accounting isn't chasing department managers for missing receipts or PO references
Have a clear audit trail for asset purchases that tracks custody from the point of receipt through reassignment or retirement
objections: If every manager has their own queue and I can't see across queues as COO, how do I catch a situation where approvals are stacking up because a manager is absent?
Escalation sounds useful in theory, but if it just fires the PO up to the CEO's queue, I'm not solving the bottleneck — I'm just moving it
Operational managers are already stretched — adding a daily login to a new system to check a queue is a habit change I'm not sure they'll sustain
If a manager is set up incorrectly in the system (wrong direct reports assigned), how quickly can an admin fix that, and what happens to POs already in the queue?
I'm worried about the 'clarification cycle' — if a PO bounces back for clarification repeatedly, it could stall for days; is there a way to track how many times a PO has been returned?
What happens to urgent purchases when the system is unavailable — do we have a documented fallback, or does everything stop?
I need to be able to reassign an aging approval to myself or another manager without the original manager being available to log in and delegate
The equipment registration feature sounds valuable, but only if the accounting team actually completes the asset metadata during receipt — what enforces that?
I'm concerned that the hold function could be misused to park inconvenient POs indefinitely — is there a way to see all POs on hold and how long they've been there?
If approvals route through me, will I get buried in low-value sign-offs instead of only the exceptions that actually need an operator’s call?
openQuestions: Show me the manager review queue from a manager's perspective — when they log in, what do they see first, and how is the queue sorted?
How does the urgent flag on a PO affect its position in the manager queue — does it go to the top automatically, or does the manager have to filter for it?
If I need to cover for the CEO on owner approvals while they're away, how does delegation actually work from a workflow perspective — does the routing change immediately?
Walk me through what happens when I escalate a PO from the manager stage to the owner stage — what does the owner see, and does the original manager still see the PO?
Can I see the full Registry filtered by department and status as a COO without having accounting-level access to the system?
How does the stage-timing report break down dwell time by approval stage — can I see which specific manager queues are consistently slow?
If a manager's direct-report assignment is wrong and their queue is empty, how quickly can an admin fix that and will it pull the correct POs in retroactively?
Walk me through the hold and resume workflow on a PO — who can put a PO on hold, who can resume it, and is there an audit record of both actions?
When a PO with asset lines is received, what does the equipment registration process look like — is it a required step before the PO can be closed?
Can I run a report showing all POs that are currently on hold, sorted by how long they've been in that state?
What does the Receive Queue look like from accounting's perspective — does it show all paid POs awaiting receipt, and can I see which ones are aging?
If a manager approves a PO and then needs to revoke that approval — for example, budget was cut — is that possible, or is an approval terminal?
How does the duplicate-vendor banner work in practice — when does it appear, and what information does it show about prior POs to that vendor?
Can I see the SLA compliance metric broken down by approval stage on the admin dashboard, or is it a single aggregate number?
Walk me through how a PO looks in the Activity timeline after it's been through a clarification cycle — can I see each back-and-forth exchange as a discrete event?

committee.name: Comptroller
interests: Ensuring every purchase order that reaches the payment stage has a complete, valid GL account on every line — the Finalize gate must be enforced, not optional
Having a documented separation-of-duties structure that prevents the person who initiates a payment from also marking it paid
Knowing that the audit trail for every PO is complete and exportable without any manual reconstruction before month-end close
Ensuring that approval bypasses are visible and logged — he needs to know what the threshold engine cleared without an owner review
Having reliable controls over who can access the GL accounts chart and make changes that affect how PO lines are coded
Tracking which POs are in the payment queue vs. in-flight vs. awaiting receipt, so he can reconcile cash disbursements accurately
Ensuring that asset-line POs create corresponding equipment records on receipt, so fixed-asset schedules don't require manual reconstruction from invoices
Having the ability to reassign aging approvals in the accounting payment queue if a staff member is absent during a critical close window
Understanding what the 'approved-conditional' status means in practice — what conditions are recorded and whether accounting staff can see them
Having a reporting view that shows authorized spend by GL account and department for the current period without a manual export every time
Ensuring that vendor payments are tied back to a specific approved PO so every disbursement has a corresponding authorization record
Knowing that the system's GL account archive function prevents deletion of accounts referenced by finalized POs, preserving the integrity of historical records
decisionCriteria: The Finalize gate structurally refuses to close a PO with any line missing a GL account — this must be demonstrably enforced, not just documented
Separation of duties on payment is real: the user who starts payment processing cannot also mark the PO paid — verified by attempting it in a demo environment
Every governed mutation (approve, reject, pay, receive, finalize, override) is captured in the Audit tab and exportable via the audit-bundle CSV
Bypassed Approvals queue shows the specific threshold-routing logic that cleared each PO, so he can answer auditor questions about missing owner sign-off
GL accounts cannot be deleted — only archived — so finalized POs never reference a code that no longer exists in the chart
The accounting payment queue separates Pending Payment from In Payment so his staff can see exactly what is waiting to be processed vs. already in flight
Asset receipts automatically create equipment records in the asset registry — he can verify this by receiving an asset-line PO in a demo
The CSV exports (audit bundle, open-PO aging, vendor spend) are formatted so his bookkeeper or external CPA can use them without reformatting
Approved-conditional POs show the recorded condition in the PO detail so accounting staff know what to verify before processing payment
The system's role model gives accounting access to GL accounts, locations, and the receive queue without requiring admin-level access to all settings
Notification rules alert accounting staff when a PO enters the payment queue, so items don't sit unprocessed because no one checked the queue
A SOD override is possible for specific situations, but the override is recorded in the audit trail — he can see who overrode what and when
goals: Close each accounting period without chasing department managers for missing PO references or undocumented purchases
Have a complete, exportable audit record for every PO that satisfies an external auditor without supplemental documentation
Eliminate the risk of a disbursement being made without a corresponding approved PO in the system
Ensure that every GL code on every finalized PO is valid and traceable to the chart of accounts at the time of finalization
Maintain a fixed-asset schedule that is populated automatically from PO receipts rather than manually from invoices
Have clear visibility into the payment pipeline — what is approved and waiting, what is in processing, what has been paid and not yet received
Enforce the organization's separation-of-duties policy structurally so it doesn't depend on individual staff following a procedure
Reduce the time his team spends per month on payment-queue management and receipt reconciliation
Ensure that conditional approvals are clearly communicated to accounting so no PO is paid without the stated condition being verified
Have a vendor spend report that shows total disbursements by vendor for the period, usable in cash-flow analysis and vendor negotiations
Ensure the organization can respond to a bank or auditor request for purchasing controls documentation within a day, not a week
Maintain GL account integrity — archived accounts must not affect finalized PO records, and new accounts must go through a controlled creation process
objections: If a manager approves a PO and then goes on leave before it reaches accounting, can I reassign the accounting-stage actions without the original approver being available — or does it stall at the payment queue?

The separation-of-duties control on payments sounds correct on paper, but what happens in a small team where the same two people handle all accounting functions — does the system have a practical workaround that is still logged?
I need to see conditional approvals before I authorize payment — if the owner records a condition in a comment field that my staff might miss, that's a control gap
How does the Finalize step interact with our accounting system — does it produce output I can import into the GL, or does my team still do manual journal entries?
If the audit-bundle CSV is the primary evidence for an auditor, I need to know it is tamper-evident — how do I know no one has edited the audit trail after the fact?
The asset registry auto-creation on receipt sounds useful, but if my team skips the asset metadata fields during receipt, the records are incomplete — what enforces completeness?
I'm concerned about a scenario where accounting staff mark a PO paid, the PO is then received, but the vendor invoice is different from the PO amount — where is that discrepancy visible?
If we archive a GL account mid-year, what happens to draft POs that already reference that account — do they fail silently or is there an explicit error?
What is the system's process for voiding or cancelling a PO that is already in the payment queue — can accounting do that, or does it require owner action?
When an auditor asks who approved a PO and why, can I pull the full approval trail in seconds, or am I reconstructing it from emails again?
openQuestions: Show me the accounting payment queue — what does my staff see for a PO in Pending Payment vs. one that's moved to In Payment, and what actions are available at each stage?
Walk me through the Finalize step — what exactly happens when I click Finalize on a fully received PO, and what does the system check before it allows it?
If a manager is out and a PO is aging in the payment queue, how does accounting escalate or reassign it without the original approver's involvement?
Demonstrate the separation-of-duties enforcement on payment — show me what happens when the user who started the payment tries to also mark it paid
What does an approved-conditional PO look like in the accounting queue — is the condition text visible to my staff before they initiate payment?
Walk me through the audit-bundle CSV for a completed PO — specifically, does it capture the bypass record if the owner stage was skipped by the threshold engine?
How does the GL accounts admin work — can my accounting staff create, edit, and archive accounts, or does that require admin access?
Show me what happens when I receive an asset-line PO — what equipment record is created, what metadata is required, and where does it appear in the asset registry?
If I archive a GL account that a draft PO already references, does the system surface an explicit error on the draft, or does it only fail at Finalize?
Can the open-PO aging CSV be scheduled or does it require a manual download each time — and what date range does it cover?
Walk me through a SOD override — when and how can it be done, who can authorize it, and what exactly does the audit trail record?
What does the vendor spend CSV show me — is it per-period by vendor, or cumulative, and does it include only paid POs or all authorized POs?
If a PO is in the payment queue and we discover the vendor is wrong, what is the correct workflow — cancel and re-raise, or can it be corrected from the detail page?
How do I configure notification rules so my accounting team gets alerted when a PO enters the payment queue without waiting for a manager to tell them?
Show me the Bypassed Approvals queue — specifically, what threshold-routing information is recorded so I can explain to an auditor why a large PO didn't require owner sign-off?

committee.name: Finance Manager
interests: Having a real-time view of authorized spend by department and budget category so she can produce variance reports without waiting for accounting to send a spreadsheet
Knowing that every PO submitted links to a budget category, so spend reports reflect actual purchasing intent rather than invoices received after the fact
Tracking which POs are at what stage so she can forecast cash requirements accurately — especially the split between approved-but-not-paid and paid-but-not-received
Having an open-PO aging report that identifies purchases that have been approved but not received within an expected window
Ensuring that the GL coding on PO lines is completed before Finalize, so her GL import reflects the correct account classifications from day one
Having a vendor-spend report that she can use as a starting point for periodic vendor review conversations with department heads
Ensuring that budget-category tagging on PO submissions is enforced, not optional — a blank budget category creates blind spots in her reporting
Understanding how the Receive Queue works so she can track the gap between authorized spend (approved POs) and recognized spend (received goods)
Having access to the asset custody CSV so she can reconcile fixed-asset records without manually cross-referencing invoices and the asset registry
Being notified when a high-value PO enters the payment queue so she can review the GL coding and conditions before payment is initiated
Knowing that duplicate vendor orders are surfaced at the point of submission, so the same purchase isn't committed twice and recorded as two separate transactions
Having the Reports spend analytics cover the correct time window — she needs to be able to pull last quarter vs. current quarter vs. trailing 90 days
decisionCriteria: The spend-by-department and spend-by-category report is accurate and current without requiring a manual data extract from accounting
Budget category is a required field on every PO submission — the wizard enforces it so her reports don't have a blanket 'uncategorized' line
Open-PO aging CSV shows POs by approved date and remaining-to-receive status so she can identify stale commitments before period close
The Reports quarter selector allows her to compare current-quarter spend to prior quarters with a single UI change, not a re-export
Vendor spend CSV breaks down disbursements by vendor so she can identify concentration risk and over-reliance on single suppliers
Asset custody CSV from Reports reconciles with the asset registry so fixed-asset reporting doesn't require a separate manual process
GL account assignment at Finalize is enforced structurally — she can demonstrate that a PO without complete GL coding cannot be finalized
The accounting payment queue separates pending from in-flight payments so she can project cash outflows for the week without calling accounting
The Registry's CSV export includes amount, department, budget category, and status so she can do ad-hoc spend analysis without a BI tool
Notification rules can be configured to alert her when a PO over a threshold enters the payment queue, giving her time to review coding before payment
The system's reporting window covers at least rolling 12 months so she can do year-over-year comparisons for budget-planning season
Her role gives her read access to all POs across departments in the Registry without requiring admin credentials
goals: Produce accurate, current budget-versus-actual reports for each department without waiting for accounting to send a spreadsheet
Forecast cash requirements accurately by knowing what is approved-and-unpaid vs. paid-and-unreceived at any point in the month
Ensure that every purchase commitment is coded to the correct GL account and budget category before it becomes a line item in the general ledger
Identify stale open POs — approved but not received within a reasonable window — so she can investigate or close them before period end
Have a vendor spend view that she can bring to a vendor review meeting without extracting and formatting data from multiple sources
Reduce the time she spends at month-end reconciling fixed-asset purchases against invoices because the asset registry is populated automatically on receipt
Ensure that conditional approvals don't result in payments being made before the stated conditions are verified by someone in finance
Have a reporting view that covers both authorized spend (approved POs) and committed spend (paid POs) so she can track both sides of the cash position
Build the habit among department managers of including accurate budget categories and cost centers on PO submissions so her reports are reliable from submission, not just at finalize
Reduce the end-of-period scramble to identify undocumented purchases by having a complete PO registry that accounts staff can reference
Have enough visibility into the receive queue to follow up on paid POs that are overdue for receipt, reducing accrual estimates at period close
Support the comptroller's audit-readiness goals by ensuring that the GL data in PO.vin and the accounting system are in sync after each Finalize event
objections: The spend reports are only useful if managers actually complete the budget category field on every submission — what happens to POs submitted with a blank category?
I need the GL coding to match our chart of accounts exactly — if a submitter can type a free-text GL account that doesn't exist in the master list, that creates reconciliation work for me
If the open-PO aging CSV only covers the current quarter, it won't help me with long-running capital commitments that span multiple periods
I'm concerned that 'approved spend' and 'authorized spend' are used differently in the Reports tiles — can you clarify exactly what each stat tile counts and excludes?
If I'm the Finance Manager but not the Comptroller, what read access do I actually have — can I see all departments' POs in the Registry, or only the ones I'm assigned to?
The vendor spend CSV is only useful for period analysis — I also need to see current open POs per vendor to manage concentration risk in real time
How does the system handle a PO where the invoice amount differs from the approved PO amount — is there any variance capture, or does that live entirely in the accounting system?
If we have a large number of POs to import at go-live, is the bulk CSV importer reliable enough for the initial data load, and what's the recovery process if the commit has errors?

I need the asset custody report to reconcile with our fixed-asset register — if the asset registry in PO.vin uses different category names than our accounting system, the reconciliation creates manual work
If a PO is raised against a budget line that’s already overcommitted, does PO.vin stop it at request time or do I only find out at month-end?
openQuestions: Show me the Reports page — specifically the spend-by-department and spend-by-category views for the current quarter, and tell me exactly what is included in the 'Authorized' stat tile vs. the 'Total Requested' tile
Walk me through the quarter selector on Reports — can I pull a comparison between Q1 and Q2 spend by department without exporting two separate CSVs?
What does the open-PO aging CSV show me — specifically, does it include POs that are approved but not yet paid, or only POs that are paid but not yet received?
If a manager submits a PO without a budget category, does the wizard reject it, or does it allow submission and flag it somewhere for review?
How does the GL account picker in the PO wizard work — is it constrained to the chart of accounts in GL Accounts Admin, or can a submitter enter a free-text value?
Show me the vendor spend CSV — does it show total authorized spend or total paid spend per vendor, and what date range does it default to?
Walk me through what happens when an asset-line PO is received — what exactly appears in the asset registry, and does the asset custody CSV on Reports reflect it immediately?
As Finance Manager with a non-admin role, what can I see in the Registry — is it all POs across all departments, or scoped to my department?
How does the system handle a situation where the same vendor submits invoices against multiple open POs — can I see all open POs for a vendor in one view?
What is the difference between a PO in 'In Accounting' status and one in 'Processing Payment' status from a cash-flow forecasting perspective?
Can I export the full Registry list — including status, department, budget category, amount, and vendor — as a CSV for ad-hoc analysis in a spreadsheet?
Show me what the accounting dashboard looks like — specifically the 'Ready for Payment' and 'Awaiting Receipt' counts and whether I can drill into each from that screen
If I need to flag a PO for finance review before payment is initiated, is there a mechanism for that within the workflow, or do I need to contact accounting directly?
How does the inventory aging CSV differ from the open-PO aging CSV — what question does each answer, and when would I use one vs. the other?
Walk me through the bulk CSV import for GL accounts — what does the dry-run output look like if I have rows with errors, and can I fix and re-run without re-uploading the whole file?
